reasoning: The base rate since the Omicron designation in late 2021 is essentially zero, with no new Variants of Concern (VOC) recognized since the updated criteria in March 2023. The new WHO criteria intentionally raise the threshold, requiring rigorous evidence that a variant demonstrates substantial clinical disease severity changes or dramatically impacts health systemic capacity out of proportion to previous variants. The ongoing evolution of SARS-CoV-2 continues to generate Variants of Interest (VOIs) and Variants under Monitoring (VUMs), such as JN.1 and XEC. However, these variants have not displayed the massive leaps in morbidity or immune evasion required to trigger a designation as a new VOC. The most recent WHO epidemic update from January 2025 mentions JN.1 as a VOI and XEC as a VUM, maintaining the current paradigm of incrementally mutating variants that evade neutralizing antibodies but do not fundamentally alter disease severity or healthcare utilization patterns on a macroeconomic scale. The forecasting community holds this event at a 2% likelihood, which aligns squarely with historical patterns and the current epidemiological landscape. Given the short time horizon until July 2025 and the absence of any early-warning signals of a highly divergent and severe variant, the probability of a new VOC designation remains extremely low. I estimate the absolute minimum but use the allowed bound of 2%.
